verses.commentary: Early morning. All the chief priests and elders take counsel against Jesus to put him to death. The night verdict is formalized in daylight — but Sanhedrin lacks the authority to execute. They need Rome.
Bind him, lead him away, deliver (paredokan, παρέδωκαν) to Pilate the governor. Paradidomi recurs — the verb tracks the chain of handing-over.
Judas — seeing Jesus condemned — feels remorse (metameletheis, μεταμεληθεὶς — change of mind without faith, distinct from saving repentance). Returns the thirty silver pieces.
'I have sinned, betraying innocent blood.' Confession to the wrong audience. 'What is that to us? See to it yourself.' The accomplices wash their hands of his conscience.
Throws the coins into the temple, departs, hangs himself. Despair-suicide. Money rejected too late.
Chief priests: 'It is not lawful (ouk exestin, οὐκ ἔξεστιν) to put them into the treasury — it is blood money.' Scruples about coin-purity while plotting murder. The irony bites.
They buy the potter's field as a burial place for foreigners. Strangers' graves are funded by Judas' silver. Poetic justice.
Field called Field of Blood (Akeldama in Aramaic, Acts 1:19) — to this day. Geographic memorial of the betrayal.

'Then was fulfilled what was spoken by Jeremiah the prophet — they took thirty pieces of silver, the price of him whose price was set...' (Quote is mostly Zechariah 11:12-13; the attribution to Jeremiah debated — perhaps Jeremiah 19's potter's field plus Zechariah 11.)
'...gave them for the potter's field, as the Lord directed me.' Composite citation. The prophetic anticipation of the betrayal price's destination.
Before Pilate. 'Are you the king of the Jews?' Roman version of the Sanhedrin's messianic question. 'You have said so.' Indirect affirmation.
Accused by chief priests and elders — Jesus gives no answer. Silence before false witness (Isaiah 53:7 — 'as a lamb led to the slaughter, he opened not his mouth').
Pilate: 'Do you not hear how many things they testify against you?' The governor surprised by the silence — silence in Roman courts implied admission, but more often invited torture.
Jesus does not answer even one charge. The governor marvels (thaumazein, θαυμάζειν). Silence as authority.
Festival custom: governor releases one prisoner to the crowd. Roman accommodation to Jewish festival.
Notable prisoner: Jesus Barabbas. Some manuscripts have his first name as 'Jesus' — making the choice 'Jesus the son of the father' (Bar-abba) or 'Jesus the Christ.' Two Jesuses, two paths.
Pilate offers the choice. Knowing they delivered Jesus out of envy, he tries political workaround.
Pilate knew the leaders' envy (phthonos, φθόνος). The governor reads the politics correctly but lacks the courage of his reading.

Pilate's wife sends warning — 'Have nothing to do with that righteous man, I have suffered much over him in a dream today.' Gentile woman's intuitive righteousness contrasts the leaders. Dreams again carry revelation (compare 1:20; 2:12).
Chief priests and elders persuade crowds to ask for Barabbas and destroy Jesus. The mob is manipulable; leadership shapes consent.
'Which of the two?' 'Barabbas.' The rebel-murderer preferred over the Messiah. Choice of the violent over the gentle.
'What then shall I do with Jesus who is called Christ?' 'Crucify him!' Capital punishment demanded for the one without charge.
'Why? What evil has he done?' Reasonable question ignored. They shout louder — 'Crucify him!' Mob noise drowns reason.

Pilate sees getting nowhere; takes water, washes hands before the crowd: 'I am innocent of this man's blood. See to it yourselves.' Symbolic abdication (Deuteronomy 21:6-9). The water cannot rinse responsibility off.

All the people: 'His blood be on us and on our children.' Self-curse historically read in many tragic ways — Matthew records it as the crowd's voluntary acceptance of legal responsibility, not as eternal covenant-judgment on Jewish people.
Released Barabbas; had Jesus scourged (phragelloo, φραγελλόω — Roman flagellation, often near-lethal) and delivered to be crucified. The pre-cross brutality severe.
Soldiers take Jesus to the praetorium — Roman headquarters. Whole cohort (speira, σπεῖρα — 600 men) gathers around him.
Strip him, put scarlet robe on him. Cheap parody of royal purple. The mock-coronation begins.
Crown of thorns plaited on his head. Reed (kalamos, κάλαμος) in right hand for scepter. Mock-homage on knees: 'Hail, King of the Jews!' Every element of royal investiture grotesquely echoed.
Spit on him. Took the reed, struck him on the head. Isaiah 50:6 fulfilled in detail.
Mockery done — strip the robe, put his own clothes back. Lead away to crucify.
Simon of Cyrene (North African Jew) — forced to carry the cross. Conscripted involuntarily; eventually Mark mentions his sons Alexander and Rufus, suggesting they became known Christians.
Golgotha — Aramaic for 'place of the skull' (kraniou topos, κρανίου τόπος). Likely named for its appearance or its execution-history. Latin: Calvaria.
Offered wine mixed with gall (cholē, χολή). Possibly intended as anesthetic; or fulfillment of Psalm 69:21. He tastes but refuses — undrugged consciousness chosen for the cross.
Crucified him. They divided his garments by casting lots (Psalm 22:18 fulfilled). The brevity of the verb 'crucified' contrasts with the elaborate prophecy-fulfillment in the surrounding details.
Sitting, they watched. The soldiers' guard duty — efficient, indifferent. Crucifixion was their job.
Charge placed above his head: 'This is Jesus, the King of the Jews.' The titulus stating the criminal accusation — and unintentionally stating the truth.
Two robbers (lestai, λῃσταί — possibly insurrectionists) crucified with him, one on right and left. Isaiah 53:12 — 'numbered with the transgressors.'
Passers-by deride him, wagging heads (Psalm 22:7). The Servant fulfills another Psalm of suffering.

'You who would destroy the temple and build it in three days, save yourself! If you are the Son of God, come down.' Temptation from 4:6 echoed — same 'if you are the Son of God' phrasing. Satan's logic in human mouths.
Chief priests, scribes, elders mock similarly. The religious establishment joins the spectacle.

'He saved others; he cannot save himself. He is the King of Israel — let him come down from the cross now, and we will believe him.' They state his mission (saves others) correctly without seeing why the cross is the saving. Their proposed evidence would have invalidated salvation.
'He trusts in God; let God deliver him now, if he wants him.' Psalm 22:8 quoted ironically — they unwittingly speak Scripture against the Scripture's subject.
Even the robbers crucified with him reproached him in the same way. (Luke 23:39-43 distinguishes the two — one repents.) Matthew gives the corporate hostility.
From the sixth hour (noon) to the ninth (3 pm) darkness over all the land. Cosmic sign — Amos 8:9-10 (the day of the Lord brings darkness at noon).

About the ninth hour Jesus cries with loud voice: 'Eli, Eli, lema sabachthani' — Psalm 22:1 in Aramaic. 'My God, my God, why have you forsaken me?' The cry interprets the cross as ultimate identification with abandonment, even as it quotes a psalm that ends in vindication.
Bystanders hear 'Eli' as 'Elijah.' Phonetic confusion. They wait to see if Elijah will rescue — same false hope mocked by the previous 'come down from the cross.'
One runs, takes a sponge, fills with sour wine on a reed, gives to drink. Psalm 69:21 fulfilled again. The simple act may be partly compassionate, partly performative.
Others: 'Wait, let us see whether Elijah comes.' Last test posed by spectators.
Jesus cries again with loud voice and 'yielded up his spirit (apheken to pneuma, ἀφῆκεν τὸ πνεῦμα).' Voluntary release. He gives up life rather than has it taken.

Temple veil torn in two from top to bottom (the direction signaling divine action). The separation between Holy Place and Most Holy Place is ended; access opened. Hebrews 10:19-22 interprets this theologically. Earth shook, rocks split — cosmic ratification.
Tombs opened. Bodies of many saints who had fallen asleep were raised. Matthew unique. Resurrection seismic.

After Jesus' resurrection, they came out, entered the holy city, appeared to many. The narrative inserts this here but dates it post-resurrection. The cross unleashes a wave of resurrections — firstfruits visible.

Centurion and those with him guarding Jesus — seeing the earthquake and what took place — terrified: 'Truly this was the Son of God.' Gentile soldier confesses what Israel's leaders rejected. Inclusion announced at the moment of death.

Many women there, looking on from a distance. They had followed Jesus from Galilee, ministering to him (diakonousai, διακονοῦσαι). The unnamed female disciples are present when the male disciples are not.
Among them: Mary Magdalene, Mary the mother of James and Joseph, the mother of Zebedee's sons. The community of women is named. Witnesses to crucifixion become witnesses to resurrection.
Evening. A rich man from Arimathea named Joseph — disciple of Jesus. Wealth and discipleship can coexist when wealth serves the cross. Compare the rich young ruler (19:22).
Goes to Pilate, asks for Jesus' body. Pilate orders it given. Roman custom often left corpses on crosses; Joseph's intervention is significant.
Joseph takes body, wraps in clean linen shroud (sindon, σινδών). Reverent burial preparation.
Lays it in his own new tomb hewn out of the rock. Isaiah 53:9: 'with a rich man in his death.' Rolls a great stone to the door. Departs. The grief is sealed in.
Mary Magdalene and the other Mary sat opposite the tomb. Watching the burial. The women bear continuous witness.
Next day (Sabbath) — chief priests and Pharisees go to Pilate. They violate their own Sabbath scruples to ensure Jesus stays buried.
'Sir, we remember how that imposter said while still alive, After three days I will rise.' They remember his words better than the disciples do. Their fear gives them better hearing.

Request: secure tomb until the third day, lest disciples steal the body and claim resurrection. 'The last fraud would be worse than the first.' Their hostility maps the expected narrative they want to prevent.
Pilate: 'You have a guard (koustodia, κουστωδία — Latin loanword). Go, make it secure as you can.' Granted the security detail.
They go, secure the tomb by sealing the stone and posting the guard. Maximum security applied. The very precautions become the evidence that the resurrection cannot be explained by theft.
